title: Festivus: The Holiday for the Rest of Us
story: Every December 23, families across America celebrate Festivus, the quirky anti-holiday that's all about keeping things simple and real. Instead of decorating a Christmas tree, Festivus fans put up a plain aluminum pole in their living room because it's totally no-fuss. The celebration includes two main traditions: first, everyone sits around the dinner table sharing their "Airing of Grievances," where they tell family members all the ways they've been disappointed that year. Then comes the "Feats of Strength," where the head of the household must be pinned in a wrestling match before Festivus can officially end. This unusual holiday started as a joke on a TV show but became so popular that real families now celebrate it as their own special tradition.
activity_question: What do people use instead of a Christmas tree during Festivus?
bonus_fact: Festivus was invented by a TV writer's dad in the 1960s before appearing on Seinfeld.

title: Happy Birthday, Your Majesty!
story: Every December 23, all of Japan celebrates their Emperor's birthday like one giant national party! Schools and offices close so families can spend time together, just like a special weekend. People hang Japanese flags outside their homes and visit the Imperial Palace in Tokyo, where thousands gather to wave at the Emperor and wish him well. Some families exchange gifts or enjoy special meals together. It's the only day of the year when regular people can actually see the Emperor up close and personal!
activity_question: What do thousands of people do when they visit the Imperial Palace on the Emperor's birthday?
activity_choices: Wave at the Emperor and wish him well
activity_answer: Wave at the Emperor and wish him well
bonus_fact: It's the only day regular citizens can see the Emperor up close!

title: Radish Art Takes Root in Mexico
story: Every December 23, the city of Oaxaca in Mexico transforms into an incredible outdoor art gallery made entirely of radishes! Artists spend weeks growing giant radishes, then carve them into amazing sculptures of people, animals, buildings, and nativity scenes. Families walk through the main square admiring hundreds of detailed radish creations displayed on tables. The carved radishes only last one night before they start to wilt, making this celebration extra special. Winners receive prizes for the most creative designs. People travel from around the world to see these vegetable masterpieces on this magical night.
activity_question: What happens to the radish sculptures after one night?
bonus_fact: Some radishes grown for this festival can weigh over 6 pounds!
